items.correct: By the word's grammatical function in a sentence — not its meaning. The same word can belong to different classes depending on use (e.g. 'run' as noun or verb).
items.misconception: A noun is always a person, place or thing.
items.random_wrong: A noun is any word that starts with a capital letter.
items.why: 'Running' can be a noun ('Running is healthy'), a verb ('I am running'), or an adjective ('running water'). The notional definition fails — function in context determines word class.

items.q: What is the difference between descriptive and prescriptive approaches to language?
items.correct: Descriptivists describe how language is actually used; prescriptivists rule on how language ought to be used — modern linguistics is descriptivist.
items.misconception: Descriptivists describe written language and prescriptivists describe spoken language.
items.random_wrong: Both terms mean the same — analysing language scientifically.
items.why: The split is about attitude, not mode. Descriptivists treat all varieties as valid; prescriptivists make value judgements. Students mistake it for a written/spoken distinction.

items.q: What is the difference between semantics and pragmatics?
items.correct: Semantics studies literal meaning of words and sentences; pragmatics studies how meaning depends on context, speaker intention and conversational implicature.
items.misconception: Semantics is grammar and pragmatics is vocabulary.
items.random_wrong: Semantics is speech and pragmatics is writing.
items.why: 'Can you pass the salt?' semantically asks ability; pragmatically requests salt. Students miss that pragmatics governs the gap between literal meaning and intended meaning.

items.q: A teenager speaking Standard English in class but slang with friends is doing what?
items.why: It's Codeswitching. “Accommodating only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Moving your speech towards your listener's
items.random_wrong: Moving your speech away from your listener's
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Moving your speech towards your listener's. “Speaking like Shakespeare” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many directions can accommodation go (convergence and divergence)?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
